algorithms.title: Drowning Resuscitation (Ventilation-Focused)
algorithms.description: The hypoxia-driven approach to a drowning victim in or near cardiac arrest.
steps: Ensure rescuer safety and remove the victim from the water (trained rescuers may begin in-water rescue breaths once safe and the victim is reachable).
On a firm surface, open the airway and give initial rescue breaths.
If pulseless, begin conventional CPR with compressions AND breaths — avoid compression-only CPR.
Dry the chest and apply an AED; follow prompts and resume compressions immediately after any shock.
Do not attempt routine maneuvers to expel water from the lungs.
Continue CPR with breaths until signs of life or handoff; transport promptly.

algorithms.title: CPR in Late Pregnancy
algorithms.description: Modifications to high-quality CPR for a pregnant patient in the second half of pregnancy.
steps: Provide high-quality CPR with standard hand placement, rate, and depth on a firm surface.
Perform manual left uterine displacement (gently push the uterus to the patient's left) to relieve compression of the major vessels.
Use the AED normally — defibrillation is safe in pregnancy.
Activate advanced resources early and transport rapidly; advanced interventions may be needed for mother and fetus.

algorithms.title: Post-ROSC Care and Recovery Position
algorithms.description: BLS priorities after return of spontaneous circulation and for the unresponsive patient who is breathing.
steps: Recognize ROSC: palpable pulse, spontaneous breathing, movement, or other signs of life.
Monitor airway, breathing, and pulse continuously; assist ventilation and give oxygen if breathing is inadequate.
Keep the AED attached in case of re-arrest and be ready to resume CPR.
For an unresponsive patient breathing normally with a pulse and no spinal injury, use the recovery position to protect the airway.
Transport promptly to definitive care and support the recovery link — survivor, family, and rescuer debriefing.
